Description:
Canada contains extensive wilderness areas spanning alpine, boreal, and Arctic environments. Hiking is primarily concentrated in protected regions, especially within the Canadian Rockies.

Western regions are defined by alpine terrain, with glacial valleys and high-elevation routes in areas such as Banff National Park. Coastal areas, including Vancouver Island, feature temperate rainforest and shoreline trails. Northern regions such as the Yukon consist of tundra and remote backcountry with limited infrastructure.

Colombia’s terrain is dominated by three Andean ranges, with additional environments including rainforest, cloud forest, and Caribbean coastline. This geographic variation supports diverse hiking conditions.

Andean regions contain high-altitude trails and cloud forest landscapes, including areas such as the Cocora Valley. Northern coastal zones, including Tayrona National Park, combine jungle terrain with shoreline routes. Inland lowland regions consist of dense tropical rainforest, with longer treks such as the route to Ciudad Perdida.

Peru’s geography includes the Andes Mountains and the western Amazon Basin, creating varied hiking environments. Many routes follow historic pathways associated with Inca infrastructure.

High-altitude Andean regions contain mountain passes and alpine terrain, including routes such as the Inca Trail and areas near Rainbow Mountain. Eastern regions transition into the Amazon Rainforest, where hiking occurs in lowland jungle environments and is often combined with river access.

Belize consists primarily of lowland tropical rainforest, karst formations, and coastal ecosystems. Hiking is concentrated inland and often intersects with archaeological sites.

Inland regions are dominated by dense rainforest with established trail systems in protected areas such as Cockscomb Basin Wildlife Sanctuary. Karst landscapes include cave systems such as Actun Tunichil Muknal. Western areas contain forested terrain with Maya sites including Caracol.

Bolivia is dominated by high-altitude Andean and plateau environments, with additional transitions into cloud forest and Amazon basin.

Western regions consist of the Altiplano, with arid high plains and volcanic terrain, alongside alpine routes in the Cordillera Real. Eastern slopes descend into the Yungas, where cloud forest trails connect high mountains to tropical lowlands.

Brazil contains diverse ecosystems including rainforest, highlands, and coastal mountain systems. Hiking environments are distributed across several distinct regions.

Northern areas are dominated by the Amazon Rainforest, characterized by dense jungle and river-based access. Southeastern regions include mountainous terrain and Atlantic Forest, with established trails in areas such as Chapada Diamantina National Park.

The Bahamas consists of low-lying limestone islands with coastal and mangrove ecosystems. Elevation is minimal but terrain varies across shoreline and karst features.

Most routes occur along beaches, dunes, and mangrove systems, including areas such as Lucayan National Park. Inland terrain is flat, with cave systems and limestone formations.

Chile spans a long north–south axis, producing extreme variation from desert to glacial environments along the Andes.

Northern regions include the Atacama Desert, with arid high-altitude terrain and volcanic features. Southern Patagonia, including Torres del Paine National Park, contains glacial systems, alpine peaks, and established trekking routes. Central regions include forested volcanic landscapes in the Lake District.

Costa Rica is characterized by tropical rainforest, cloud forest, and volcanic landscapes. Hiking is concentrated in protected reserves.

Lowland regions consist of dense rainforest, including areas such as Corcovado National Park. Higher elevations include cloud forest environments in Monteverde Cloud Forest Reserve, while volcanic regions such as Arenal Volcano provide varied terrain and elevation gradients.

Cuba combines coastal plains, karst landscapes, and mountainous interiors. Hiking is concentrated in protected natural areas.

Western regions include limestone formations and valleys such as Vińales Valley. Eastern regions contain the island’s primary ??????? terrain in the Sierra Maestra, with forested trails and higher elevations.

Dominica is a volcanic island characterized by steep terrain, rainforest, and geothermal features. It has one of the most extensive hiking networks in the Caribbean.

Interior regions consist of mountainous rainforest, including Morne Trois Pitons National Park. The Waitukubuli National Trail traverses the island longitudinally, connecting coastal and inland environments.

The Dominican Republic includes coastal plains, rainforest, and mountainous interior regions. Hiking is concentrated in national parks and upland areas.

Central regions are defined by the Cordillera Central, including routes to Pico Duarte. Coastal and southwestern regions include drier forests and protected coastal terrain.

Ecuador is divided into Andean highlands, Amazon rainforest, and coastal lowlands. Hiking is concentrated in the Andes and protected reserves.

Highland regions include volcanic terrain and crater lakes, with routes in areas such as Cotopaxi National Park. Eastern regions descend into the Amazon Rainforest, with jungle-based hiking environments.

Guatemala features volcanic highlands, cloud forest, and lowland rainforest. Hiking environments vary significantly by elevation.

Highland regions include active volcanoes and crater lakes, such as routes near Acatenango Volcano and Lake Atitlán. Northern lowlands consist of tropical rainforest, including archaeological zones such as Tikal.

Antigua and Barbuda consist of low-lying islands with coastal, scrub, and dry forest environments. Elevation is limited, but shoreline terrain is varied.

Antigua features rolling hills and coastal trails, including routes around areas such as Shirley Heights. Barbuda is flatter, with extensive beaches, lagoons, and minimal inland trail development.

Anguilla is a low-lying limestone island with predominantly coastal and scrub environments. Hiking is informal and limited in elevation.

Most routes follow shorelines, rocky outcrops, and sandy beaches. Inland terrain is flat, with sparse vegetation and minimal established trail systems.

Aruba is characterized by arid, desert-like terrain with cacti, rocky hills, and coastal cliffs. Hiking is concentrated in protected natural areas.

Interior regions, including Arikok National Park, contain dry scrubland and rocky trails. Coastal areas feature exposed limestone formations and shoreline routes.

Barbados consists of coral limestone terrain with coastal cliffs and inland gullies. Hiking is limited but varied in terrain.

Eastern regions along the Atlantic coast feature rugged cliffs and rock formations near areas such as Bathsheba. Inland areas include shaded gullies and light forest cover with informal trails.

Saint Barthélemy is a small, hilly island with dry vegetation and coastal environments. Hiking is informal but accessible due to compact geography.

Interior hills provide short routes with elevation gain and coastal views. Shoreline areas include rocky beaches and protected natural reserves.

Bermuda is composed of low limestone islands with coastal, wetland, and subtropical vegetation. Hiking is supported by established walking routes.

The Bermuda Railway Trail provides a continuous path across the islands. Coastal and inland reserves include wetlands, caves, and forested sections.

Panama consists of tropical rainforest, highlands, and coastal environments connecting Central and South America. Hiking is concentrated in protected forest and upland regions.

Central regions include lowland rainforest with established trails in areas such as Soberanía National Park. Western highlands rise to volcanic peaks, including routes on Volcán Barú. Eastern regions such as Darién are characterized by dense, remote rainforest with minimal infrastructure.

Nicaragua features volcanic terrain, rainforest, and large freshwater lakes. Hiking environments are shaped by active and dormant volcanoes.

Western regions include volcanic chains with routes such as Cerro Negro. Central areas contain cloud forest and reserves such as Mombacho Volcano. Southern regions around Ometepe Island combine volcanic slopes with lake-based access.

Honduras consists of mountainous interior regions and tropical lowlands. Hiking is concentrated in national parks and forest reserves.

Interior highlands include cloud forest environments such as Celaque National Park. Northern regions include rainforest terrain in Pico Bonito National Park, while western areas contain forested archaeological zones such as Copán.

El Salvador is defined by volcanic terrain, mountainous uplands, and Pacific coastal plains. Hiking environments are compact but varied.

Central and western regions include active volcanoes, with routes such as Santa Ana Volcano. Highland areas include forested regions along the Ruta de las Flores, while coastal regions are lower and less developed for hiking.

Paraguay consists of subtropical forest, grassland, and semi-arid regions. Hiking is limited but present in protected areas.

Eastern regions contain Atlantic Forest remnants with trails in areas such as Ybycuí National Park. Western regions form part of the Gran Chaco, characterized by flat, dry forest and sparse trail infrastructure.

Uruguay is characterized by rolling grasslands, low mountains, and coastal environments. Elevation is limited, but terrain varies between inland and coastal regions.

Interior areas include gentle hills and rural landscapes, with protected ????? such as Quebrada de los Cuervos. Eastern coastal regions feature cliffs, dunes, and shoreline walking routes.

Venezuela includes Andean mountains, tropical rainforest, and unique plateau formations known as tepuis. Hiking environments vary significantly by region.

Western regions contain Andean terrain with mountainous routes near Mérida. Southeastern regions include the Gran Sabana, characterized by tepui formations such as Mount Roraima. Lowland rainforest areas include routes to features such as Angel Falls.

Guyana is dominated by tropical rainforest and savanna, with limited but remote hiking opportunities. Access is often dependent on guided travel.

Interior regions consist of dense rainforest and river systems, with major destinations such as Kaieteur Falls. Southern and western areas include mixed savanna and forest environments with minimal infrastructure.

Suriname is heavily forested, with most of its land covered by tropical rainforest. Hiking is limited and typically conducted within protected reserves.

Interior regions include areas such as Central Suriname Nature Reserve, characterized by dense forest and river access. Trails are sparse and often require guided entry.

Mexico includes desert, mountainous highlands, tropical forest, and coastal environments. Hiking conditions vary significantly by region.

Northern regions are dominated by arid and canyon landscapes, including areas such as Copper Canyon. Central regions include volcanic mountainous terrain, with peaks such as Pico de Orizaba. Southern regions transition into tropical forest, including jungle environments near Chiapas.

Greenland is characterized by Arctic tundra, ice sheet margins, and coastal fjord systems. Hiking is seasonal and largely undeveloped.

Coastal regions provide access to tundra and fjord landscapes, particularly near settlements. Inland areas are dominated by the Greenland Ice Sheet, with limited accessibility and no formal trail infrastructure.

Alaska consists of Arctic tundra, mountain systems, glaciers, and coastal rainforest. Hiking is often remote and minimally developed.

Interior regions include tundra and mountain environments such as Denali National Park. Southern coastal areas include rainforest and fjord landscapes, while much of the state consists of off-trail backcountry terrain.

The contiguous United States contains a wide range of environments, including mountains, desert, forest, and coastline. Hiking infrastructure is extensive and highly developed.

Western regions include mountainous and desert terrain, with major systems such as the Rockies and canyon landscapes. Eastern regions are characterized by forested mountain trails, including long-distance routes such as the Appalachian Trail. Trail systems range from short park routes to extensive backcountry networks.

Hawaii consists of volcanic islands with tropical rainforest, coastal cliffs, and lava landscapes. Hiking environments are shaped by volcanic activity and climate variation.

Volcanic regions include active and dormant terrain such as Hawai?i Volcanoes National Park. Coastal areas feature steep cliffs and shoreline trails, while inland regions include rainforest and high-elevation crater landscapes.

The Falkland Islands consist of subantarctic grassland, rocky coastline, and low mountain terrain. Hiking is informal and largely off-trail.

Coastal regions feature cliffs and wildlife habitats. Inland areas include open grassland and rolling terrain with minimal trail infrastructure.

French Guiana is predominantly covered by tropical rainforest, forming part of the Amazon Basin. Hiking opportunities are limited and often remote.

Interior regions consist of dense forest with river-based access, including protected areas such as Guiana Amazonian Park. Trails are sparse and typically require guided travel.

Jamaica includes mountainous interior regions, rainforest, and karst landscapes. Hiking is concentrated in upland and protected areas.

Eastern regions are defined by the Blue Mountains, with high-elevation trails and forest cover in areas such as Blue and John Crow Mountains National Park. Western regions include karst terrain in the Cockpit Country, while lowland areas contain rainforest and river systems.

Haiti is characterized by mountainous terrain with limited forest cover and coastal plains. Hiking infrastructure is minimal.

Interior regions include mountain ranges such as the Massif de la Selle, with routes to peaks including Pic la Selle. Coastal areas are lower and more sparsely developed for hiking.

Puerto Rico consists of tropical rainforest, central mountains, and coastal environments. Hiking is well-developed in protected areas.

Eastern regions include rainforest trails in El Yunque National Forest. Central regions contain mountainous terrain in the Cordillera Central, while coastal areas include cliffs and shoreline routes.

Saint Kitts and Nevis are volcanic islands with rainforest-covered slopes and central peaks. Hiking is focused on interior mountainous terrain.

Saint Kitts includes routes on Mount Liamuiga, while Nevis features trails ascending Nevis Peak. Coastal areas are lower and less developed for hiking.

Saint Lucia is defined by volcanic peaks, rainforest, and coastal environments. Hiking is concentrated in mountainous interior regions.

Southern regions include steep volcanic terrain such as Gros Piton. Interior areas consist of rainforest trails and waterfall systems, while coastal areas provide limited hiking routes.

Saint Vincent and the Grenadines include a mountainous main island and low-lying smaller islands. Hiking is concentrated on Saint Vincent.

Interior regions include volcanic terrain with routes to La Soufričre. The Grenadines are flatter, with limited inland hiking and primarily coastal walking routes.

Trinidad and Tobago consist of rainforest-covered mountains and coastal environments. Hiking is concentrated in forest reserves.

Trinidad includes the Northern Range, with rainforest trails and elevation gain. Tobago features protected forest such as the Main Ridge Forest Reserve, with established trails through primary forest.

The British Virgin Islands consist of hilly islands with dry vegetation and coastal environments. Hiking is informal but accessible.

Interior hills provide short routes with elevation and coastal views, particularly on islands such as Virgin Gorda. Coastal areas include rock formations such as The Baths.

The U.S. Virgin Islands include hilly terrain, tropical forest, and coastal environments. Hiking is concentrated in protected park areas.

St. John contains the majority of established trails within Virgin Islands National Park. Trails combine inland forest routes with coastal access and historical sites.

The Cayman Islands are low-lying with limestone, mangrove, and wetland environments. Hiking is limited and generally low elevation.

Grand Cayman includes inland routes such as the Mastic Trail. Terrain is flat, with trails passing through forest, mangrove, and wetland ecosystems.

Turks and Caicos consists of low-lying limestone islands with dry scrub and coastal environments. Hiking is limited and informal.

Most routes follow coastal terrain, including dunes and rocky shoreline. Inland areas are flat with sparse vegetation and minimal trail development.

Saint Pierre and Miquelon consist of subarctic islands with grassland, bog, and coastal environments. Hiking is informal but accessible.

Coastal regions include cliffs and marine landscapes, while inland areas contain open terrain with limited elevation and minimal infrastructure.

Grenada is a volcanic island with rainforest, mountainous interior, and coastal environments. Hiking is concentrated inland.

Interior regions include forested terrain and crater lakes, with trails in areas such as Grand Etang National Park. Coastal areas are lower and less developed for hiking.

Saint Martin (French) and Sint Maarten (Dutch) share a single island with hilly terrain and coastal environments. Hiking is informal but accessible.

Interior regions include hills with trails such as those leading to Pic Paradis. Coastal areas include cliffs, beaches, and short walking routes.
